title: Credential Format Helper Guide
summary: Use this guide to understand credential format capacity, numbering headroom, and why facility-code and card-number choices can create future collision or migration risk.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: Credential Format Helper estimates whether the selected credential format leaves enough numbering space for the expected badge population.
It is intended for early planning and migration review, especially when deciding whether a site should keep a small legacy format or move to a larger format before growth creates numbering pressure.

sections.title: When to use it
bullets: Before ordering a large batch of cards or credentials.
When checking whether a legacy format still has enough room for future badge growth.
When planning multi-site or multi-tenant numbering ranges.
When comparing printed decimal numbering against bit-length-based formats.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These headings match the visible input labels on the calculator so the guide can be read directly against the form.

items.label: Facility Code Digits
items.description: The number of decimal digits allocated to the facility code portion of the credential. More digits usually means more possible facility-code values, but the actual valid range still depends on the credential format and access-control platform.
examples: 3 digits allows a planning range of roughly 000 through 999.
A site using only one facility code may not need many facility-code digits, but multi-site or tenant-separated systems may.
items.whyItMatters: Facility-code range affects how easily credentials can be partitioned by site, tenant, building, or migration group.
items.commonMistake: Assuming every printed facility-code value is valid for every panel, reader, or credential format without checking the platform.

items.label: Card Number Digits
items.description: The number of decimal digits allocated to the card-number portion of the credential. This is the part that usually carries the individual badge number within a facility-code range.
examples: 5 digits allows a planning range of roughly 00000 through 99999.
A higher badge population needs more card-number headroom, especially when replacement cards and future growth are considered.
items.whyItMatters: Card-number capacity is usually the most direct limit on how many unique badges can be issued before overlap or collision risk appears.
items.commonMistake: Sizing only for today’s active users and forgetting spare badges, replacements, contractors, inactive records, and future expansion.

items.label: Format Type
items.description: The planning method used by the calculator. Decimal uses printed facility-code and card-number digit capacity. Binary estimates capacity from the selected bit length after a basic parity allowance.
examples: Decimal is useful when the site thinks in printed facility-code and card-number ranges.
Binary is useful when comparing common credential bit lengths such as 26-bit, 34-bit, 37-bit, or 48-bit.
items.whyItMatters: The same badge population can look comfortable or risky depending on whether the real constraint is printed number range, bit length, platform support, or a manufacturer-specific format.
items.commonMistake: Treating a bit-length label as a complete credential format definition. Bit length alone does not describe every manufacturer or controller-specific field.

links.label: Reader Type Selector guide
links.note: Use this as a related guide when credential format choices also depend on reader technology, interface, or compatibility.

items.label: Bit Length (if Binary)
items.description: The selected credential bit length used for approximate binary capacity planning. The calculator applies a simple usable-bit estimate and does not decode manufacturer-specific credential structures.
examples: 26-bit is a common legacy-style planning reference.
34-bit, 37-bit, and 48-bit formats usually provide more numbering space, depending on how the format is structured.
items.whyItMatters: Bit length affects theoretical capacity, but real-world compatibility still depends on reader support, controller support, card programming, and site standards.
items.commonMistake: Assuming a larger bit length automatically works with every reader, panel, or card population already deployed.

items.label: Estimated Badge Population
items.description: The expected number of unique credentials that must fit inside the selected format. This should include active users plus realistic growth, spares, replacement badges, contractors, tenants, and migration overlap where applicable.
examples: A 500-person site may need more than 500 usable numbers once spares and replacements are included.
A multi-tenant site may need separate numbering headroom for each tenant or facility-code block.
items.whyItMatters: Population drives the capacity-used percentage and determines whether the selected format has comfortable headroom or is approaching collision risk.
items.commonMistake: Using only current employee count as the badge population and ignoring future growth, inactive records, temporary cards, and migration overlap.

links.label: Access Level Sizing guide
links.note: Use this as a related guide when badge population also affects role, schedule, and permission-structure complexity.

sections.title: How to interpret results
bullets: Capacity Used shows how much of the selected credential space is consumed by the estimated badge population.
Lower utilization means more numbering headroom and less migration pressure.
WATCH or RISK results do not automatically mean the design fails, but they do mean the format should be reviewed before rollout.
The engineering interpretation is more important than the raw capacity number because it explains how growth and migration pressure affect the decision.

sections.title: Common mistakes
bullets: Forgetting spare credentials, replacement cards, temporary badges, and inactive records.
Mixing credential formats without documenting how the access-control platform interprets them.
Assuming printed numbers and controller-decoded numbers always mean the same thing.
Choosing a format based only on today’s population instead of the planned lifecycle of the system.

sections.title: Assumptions and limits
bullets: Decimal capacity is planning math based on facility-code digits multiplied by card-number digits.
Binary capacity is approximate and uses a basic parity allowance.
This tool does not decode proprietary manufacturer credential structures.
Final credential decisions should be verified against the access-control platform, reader compatibility, card vendor, and site operating policy.
